preamble.summary: Job pauses from answering his friends and begins a monologue addressed to God and to the cosmos. In chapter 29 he looks backward, describing his former life in lavish detail: God watched over him, his lamp shone on his head, he walked through darkness by divine light, his children surrounded him, the rock poured out streams of oil. He sat as the chief man of his city, and when he spoke the elders fell silent. The young men hid themselves in respect; the nobles pressed their lips together. He rescued the poor, the fatherless, the widow, the blind, the lame, the stranger. He put on righteousness like a garment and wore justice like a turban. He expected to die in his nest, to multiply his days like sand. This is not nostalgia — it is evidence. Job is building a legal case. Before the suffering, the covenant was working. God blessed, the community honored, the vulnerable were protected. If the covenant was functioning, then the suffering that followed cannot be punishment for hidden sin.
preamble.remarkable: Job's self-portrait in chapter 29 is one of the most detailed descriptions of a righteous life in the Hebrew Bible. It parallels the ideal king of Psalm 72 and the valiant woman of Proverbs 31 — yet Job is neither a king nor a woman but a private citizen whose righteousness operated in the public square. His description of the city gate (verses 7-10) places him at the center of civic life, where legal disputes were settled and community standards were enforced. The imagery of light dominates: God's lamp on his head (v3), walking through darkness by God's light (v3), the days of his harvest or autumn (v4). The Hebrew word sod in verse 4 — often translated 'secret' — means the intimate counsel of God, the same word used in Psalm 25:14 ('the secret of the LORD is with those who fear him'). Job is claiming he once had access to God's inner circle. The chapter ends with a remarkable metaphor: Job sat among his people like a king among his troops, like one who comforts mourners (v25). He was the source of consolation for others — now he is the one who needs comforting, and no one can provide it.
preamble.friction: Job's glowing self-description raises the question of whether he is idealizing his past or reporting accurately. His friends might hear this as arrogance. But the narrator of chapters 1-2 has already confirmed Job's righteousness — the reader knows his self-assessment is not self-deception. The tension is that Job's former blessed life makes his current suffering more inexplicable, not less. If he had been secretly wicked, the suffering would make sense within the retribution framework. Instead, Job's very goodness is the foundation of his complaint. The Hebrew of verse 18 is difficult: 'I shall die in my nest' uses the word qen (nest), but 'I shall multiply my days like the chol' — chol can mean 'sand' or 'phoenix.' The phoenix reading (which appears in some rabbinic sources) would add a resurrection motif: Job expected to be renewed like the mythical bird that dies and rises again.
preamble.connections: Job's description of himself as one who was 'eyes to the blind and feet to the lame' (v15) anticipates Jesus's response to John the Baptist in Matthew 11:5: 'the blind receive sight, the lame walk.' Job's social ethic — rescuing the fatherless, defending the stranger, breaking the jaw of the wicked (v17) — parallels the prophetic demands of Isaiah 1:17 and Micah 6:8. The image of wearing righteousness as a garment (v14) appears again in Isaiah 61:10 and Ephesians 6:14. Job's lament that he expected to die in his nest connects to the wisdom tradition's promise that the righteous will have long life and peaceful death (Proverbs 3:16). The entire chapter functions as the 'before' portrait that makes chapters 30-31 devastating by contrast.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣סֶף אִ֭יּוֹב שְׂאֵ֥ת מְשָׁל֗וֹ וַיֹּאמַֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Moreover Job continued his parable, and said,
verses.rendering: And Job took up his discourse again and said:
translator_notes: The phrase se'et mashalo ('took up his parable/discourse') signals a formal speech, not casual conversation. The word mashal can mean proverb, parable, or discourse — here it introduces a sustained poetic monologue that will run through chapter 31.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־יִתְּנֵ֥נִי כְיַרְחֵי־קֶ֑דֶם כִּ֝ימֵ֗י אֱל֣וֹהַּ יִשְׁמְרֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Oh that I were as in months past, as in the days when God preserved me;
verses.rendering:
If only I could return to the months gone by,
to the days when God watched over me —
translator_notes: Mi yittaneni ('who will give me') is the standard Hebrew formula for an unrealizable wish — 'if only.' The phrase yarche-qedem ('months of old') sets the temporal frame: Job is reaching back into a past that feels impossibly distant. Eloah yishmereni ('God guarded me') uses the same verb applied to a watchman or shepherd guarding a flock.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּהִלּ֣וֹ נֵ֭רוֹ עֲלֵ֣י רֹאשִׁ֑י לְ֝אוֹר֗וֹ אֵ֣לֶךְ חֹֽשֶׁךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: When his candle shined upon my head, and when by his light I walked through darkness;
verses.rendering:
when his lamp blazed above my head,
when by his light I walked through darkness,
translator_notes: The ner (lamp) on Job's head is a metaphor for divine favor and guidance — the same image appears in Psalm 18:28 ('you light my lamp'). Walking through choshekh (darkness) by God's light suggests that even in difficult seasons, God's presence made the way navigable. The lamp is God's, not Job's — the source of illumination was always external.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּאֲשֶׁ֣ר הָ֭יִיתִי בִּימֵ֣י חׇרְפִּ֑י בְּס֥וֹד אֱ֝ל֗וֹהַּ עֲלֵ֣י אׇהֳלִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: As I was in the days of my youth, when the secret of God was upon my tabernacle;
verses.rendering:
as I was in the days of my prime,
when God's intimate counsel rested on my tent,
verses.expanded_rendering: The Hebrew chorpi can mean 'autumn' (the season of harvest and abundance) or 'prime/vigor.' The word sod means not merely 'secret' but 'intimate counsel, inner circle' — the same word used in Psalm 25:14 and Amos 3:7 for God's confidential relationship with those who fear him. Job is claiming that God once treated him as a trusted confidant, sharing his presence and purposes over Job's household. The tent (ohel) represents not just a dwelling but the entire sphere of family life and identity.
translator_notes: Sod Eloah ('counsel of God') is the theological center of this verse. In the wisdom tradition, access to God's sod is the highest form of relationship — it means being brought into God's deliberative circle. Job once had this access; now God hides his face. The loss is not merely of blessings but of intimacy.

key_terms.semantic_range: secret, counsel, confidential circle, intimate assembly
key_terms.note: Sod describes the inner circle of trust between God and a person. It appears in prophetic contexts (Amos 3:7 — God does nothing without revealing his sod to the prophets) and in wisdom contexts (Psalm 25:14). Job's claim to have had God's sod is a claim to prophetic-level intimacy.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּע֣וֹד שַׁ֭דַּי עִמָּדִ֑י סְבִ֖יבוֹתַ֣י נְעָרָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: When the Almighty was yet with me, when my children were about me;
verses.rendering:
when the Almighty was still with me,
when my children surrounded me,
translator_notes: Shaddai immadi ('the Almighty was with me') uses the divine name that dominates Job's poetry. The ne'arai ('my young ones, my children') surrounding him evokes the completeness of family life — the same children whose death is reported in chapter 1. The word 'still' (od) carries enormous weight: it implies a time before God withdrew.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּרְחֹ֣ץ הֲלִיכַ֣י בְּחֵמָ֑ה וְ֝צ֗וּר יָצ֥וּק עִ֝מָּדִ֗י פַּלְגֵי־שָֽׁמֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: When I washed my steps with butter, and the rock poured me out rivers of oil;
verses.rendering:
when my path was bathed in cream,
and the rock poured out streams of oil beside me —
translator_notes: The imagery is hyperbolic: rachots halikhai be-chemah ('bathing my steps in cream/curds') and the tsur (rock) pouring out streams of shemen (oil) describe agricultural abundance so extreme it reads as mythic. Similar imagery appears in Deuteronomy 32:13 ('he made him suck honey from the rock, and oil from the flinty rock'). The point is that creation itself participated in blessing Job.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭צֵאתִי שַׁ֣עַר עֲלֵי־קָ֑רֶת בָּ֝רְח֗וֹב אָכִ֥ין מוֹשָׁבִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: When I went out to the gate through the city, when I prepared my seat in the street!
verses.rendering:
When I went out to the gate of the city,
when I took my seat in the public square,
translator_notes: The sha'ar (gate) was the civic center of an ancient Near Eastern city — the place where legal cases were heard, business was conducted, and community leaders gathered. Job's seat (moshav) in the rechov (broad plaza) indicates his status as a leading citizen and judge. This is the beginning of a detailed social portrait that runs through verse 25.

verses.text_hebrew: רָא֣וּנִי נְעָרִ֣ים וְנֶחְבָּ֑אוּ וִֽ֝ישִׁישִׁ֗ים קָ֣מוּ עָמָֽדוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The young men saw me, and hid themselves: and the aged arose, and stood up.
verses.rendering:
the young men saw me and stepped aside;
the elders rose and stood.
translator_notes: The ne'arim (young men) hiding and the yeshishim (aged men) rising describe a society structured by honor and deference. The young hide not from fear but from respect — they make way for Job. The elders stand, which in the ancient world was a mark of extraordinary honor given to a peer or superior. Job's entrance transforms the posture of the entire assembly.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׂ֭רִים עָצְר֣וּ בְמִלִּ֑ים וְ֝כַ֗ף יָשִׂ֥ימוּ לְפִיהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The princes refrained talking, and laid their hand upon their mouth.
verses.rendering:
Nobles held back their words
and placed a hand over their mouths.
translator_notes: The sarim (princes, officials, nobles) restraining their speech and covering their mouths is a gesture of deference — they yield the floor to Job. The hand on the mouth appears again in 40:4 when Job finally responds to God's whirlwind speech. Here, others silence themselves before Job; later, Job will silence himself before God.

verses.text_hebrew: ק֤וֹל־נְגִידִ֣ים נֶחְבָּ֑אוּ וּ֝לְשׁוֹנָ֗ם לְחִכָּ֥ם דָּבֵֽקָה׃
verses.text_kjv: The nobles held their peace, and their tongue cleaved to the roof of their mouth.
verses.rendering:
The voices of leaders fell silent;
their tongues clung to the roofs of their mouths.
translator_notes: The negidim (leaders, rulers) are literally 'those who are in front.' Their tongues clinging to their palate is a vivid image of enforced silence — they could not and would not speak over Job. The escalation from young men to elders to nobles to leaders shows the totality of Job's social authority.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י אֹ֣זֶן שָׁ֭מְעָה וַתְּאַשְּׁרֵ֑נִי וְעַ֥יִן רָ֝אֲתָ֗ה וַתְּעִידֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: When the ear heard me, then it blessed me; and when the eye saw me, it gave witness to me:
verses.rendering:
Every ear that heard me called me blessed;
every eye that saw me spoke as my witness,
translator_notes: The ear and the eye function as synecdoche — the whole person represented by the organs of perception. The verb te'ashereni ('declared me blessed, fortunate') is related to the word ashre ('blessed') that opens the Psalter. The verb te'ideni ('testified for me, bore witness') is legal language — those who observed Job's life would testify on his behalf.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אֲ֭מַלֵּט עָנִ֣י מְשַׁוֵּ֑עַ וְ֝יָת֗וֹם וְֽלֹא־עֹזֵ֥ר לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Because I delivered the poor that cried, and the fatherless, and him that had none to help him.
verses.rendering:
because I rescued the poor who cried out,
the fatherless who had no one to help,
translator_notes: The ki ('because') is crucial — it explains why Job received honor. His status was not inherited or purchased but earned through justice. The ani (poor, afflicted) and yatom (fatherless, orphan) are the two most vulnerable categories in Israelite social law. Job's rescue of them mirrors the divine role described in Psalm 68:5 ('father of the fatherless').

verses.text_hebrew: בִּרְכַּ֣ת אֹ֭בֵד עָלַ֣י תָּבֹ֑א וְלֵ֖ב אַלְמָנָ֣ה אַרְנִֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: The blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me: and I caused the widow's heart to sing for joy.
verses.rendering:
The blessing of the one about to perish came upon me,
and I made the widow's heart sing.
translator_notes: The oved ('the perishing one, the one about to be destroyed') is someone on the brink of ruin — and their birkat (blessing) rests on Job. The almanah (widow) whose heart Job causes to sing (arnin) represents the most economically vulnerable person in the ancient world. Job's social ethic operates at the margins, not just among the powerful.

verses.text_hebrew: צֶ֣דֶק לָ֭בַשְׁתִּי וַיִּלְבָּשֵׁ֑נִי כִּֽמְעִ֥יל וְ֝צָנִ֗יף מִשְׁפָּטִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I put on righteousness, and it clothed me: my judgment was as a robe and a diadem.
verses.rendering:
I wore righteousness, and it clothed me;
justice was my robe and my turban.
verses.expanded_rendering: The double action — 'I put on righteousness and it clothed me' — suggests a mutual relationship between Job and tsedeq: he chose it, and it in turn defined him. The me'il (robe) is the garment of authority worn by priests and kings, and the tsanif (turban, headwrap) is a mark of dignity and office. Job is saying that his identity was inseparable from his practice of mishpat (justice). The clothing metaphor will be devastatingly reversed in chapter 30, where God strips Job and throws him into the mud.
translator_notes: Tsedeq (righteousness) and mishpat (justice) form the classic biblical word pair that defines right relationship with God and neighbor. Job does not merely practice righteousness — he wears it. The me'il is the same garment type as the priestly robe of Exodus 28:31 and Samuel's robe in 1 Samuel 2:19. The tsanif (turban) appears in Isaiah 62:3 as a royal diadem.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, rightness, justice, what is right and fitting
key_terms.note: Tsedeq in Job's usage encompasses both vertical relationship (right standing before God) and horizontal practice (just treatment of others). The clothing metaphor makes righteousness not an abstract quality but a visible, tangible identity.

key_terms.semantic_range: justice, judgment, legal decision, right order, custom
key_terms.note: Mishpat here means the active practice of rendering fair decisions and protecting the vulnerable. It is the public, judicial expression of the inner quality of tsedeq.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵינַ֣יִם הָ֭יִיתִי לַֽעִוֵּ֑ר וְרַגְלַ֖יִם לַפִּסֵּ֣חַ אָֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I was eyes to the blind, and feet was I to the lame.
verses.rendering:
I was eyes to the blind
and feet to the lame.
translator_notes: Job did not merely help the blind and lame — he became their eyes and feet. The metaphor goes beyond charity to identification: Job supplied what they lacked by giving of himself. This language anticipates the messianic signs of Isaiah 35:5-6 ('the eyes of the blind shall be opened, the lame shall leap like a deer') and Jesus's summary of his ministry in Matthew 11:5.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֣ב אָ֭נֹכִי לָֽאֶבְיוֹנִ֑ים וְרִ֖ב לֹא־יָדַ֣עְתִּי אֶחְקְרֵֽהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: I was a father to the poor: and the cause which I knew not I searched out.
verses.rendering:
I was a father to the destitute,
and the case of the stranger I investigated thoroughly.
translator_notes: Av anokhi la-evyonim ('I was a father to the needy') places Job in the divine role — God is repeatedly called father of the fatherless. The second line describes judicial diligence: riv lo yadati echqerehu ('a case I did not know, I searched it out'). Job did not judge only cases brought to him; he actively investigated injustice, even for people he did not know.

verses.text_hebrew: וָֽ֭אֲשַׁבְּרָה מְתַלְּע֣וֹת עַוָּ֑ל וּ֝מִשִּׁנָּ֗יו אַשְׁלִ֥יךְ טָֽרֶף׃
verses.text_kjv: And I brake the jaws of the wicked, and plucked the spoil out of his teeth.
verses.rendering:
I broke the fangs of the wicked
and tore the prey from his teeth.
translator_notes: The imagery shifts from pastoral care to violent confrontation. The metale'ot (jaw teeth, fangs) of the avval (wicked, unjust one) portray the oppressor as a predatory animal. Job ashlikk taref ('hurled the prey from his teeth') — he physically intervened to rescue victims from exploitation. This is not passive righteousness but aggressive justice.

verses.text_hebrew: וָ֭אֹמַר עִם־קִנִּ֣י אֶגְוָ֑ע וְ֝כַח֗וֹל אַרְבֶּ֥ה יָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Then I said, I shall die in my nest, and I shall multiply my days as the sand.
verses.rendering:
I thought, I will die in my own nest,
and I will multiply my days like the sand —
verses.expanded_rendering: Job expected the covenant to deliver on its promise: a long, full life ending peacefully at home among family. The qen (nest) is tender and domestic — the image of a bird settling in safety. The word chol is debated: it most naturally means 'sand' (countless days), but some ancient interpreters read it as 'phoenix,' the mythical bird that rises from its own ashes. If the phoenix reading is correct, Job expected not just long life but renewal — a hope that makes his current devastation even more bitter.
translator_notes: Im qinni ('with my nest') uses the word for a bird's nest, evoking security, family, and home. The verb egva ('I will expire, breathe my last') is a gentle word for dying — natural death rather than violent end. The ambiguity of chol (sand or phoenix) has generated extensive discussion. The Septuagint translates it as 'palm tree' (long-lived), some rabbinic commentators read 'phoenix.' The simplest reading is 'sand' — innumerable days.

verses.text_hebrew: שׇׁרְשִׁ֣י פָת֣וּחַ אֲלֵי־מָ֑יִם וְ֝טַ֗ל יָלִ֥ין בִּקְצִירִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: My root was spread out by the waters, and the dew lay all night upon my branch.
verses.rendering:
my roots spread open to the water,
and dew rested all night on my branches.
translator_notes: Job compares himself to a well-watered tree — the same image used for the righteous person in Psalm 1:3 and Jeremiah 17:8. The shorshi (my root) reaching water and the tal (dew) settling on his qatsir (branch, harvest) describe a life of perpetual vitality. This is the opposite of the wicked person's fate in Job 18:16 ('his roots dry up below').

verses.text_hebrew: כְּ֭בוֹדִי חָדָ֣שׁ עִמָּדִ֑י וְ֝קַשְׁתִּ֗י בְּיָדִ֥י תַחֲלִֽיף׃
verses.text_kjv: My glory was fresh in me, and my bow was renewed in my hand.
verses.rendering:
My honor was always fresh within me,
and my bow renewed itself in my hand.
translator_notes: Kevodi chadash ('my glory was new/fresh') suggests a reputation that never grew stale or faded. The qeshti (my bow) is a symbol of strength and virility. The verb tachalif ('it renewed itself, was replaced fresh') implies that Job's power was not diminishing with age but continually refreshed — a sign of ongoing divine favor.

verses.text_hebrew: לִֽי־שָׁמְע֥וּ וְיִחֵ֑לּוּ וְ֝יִדְּמ֗וּ לְמ֣וֹ עֲצָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Unto me men gave ear, and waited, and kept silence at my counsel.
verses.rendering:
People listened to me and waited;
they kept silent for my counsel.
translator_notes: The three verbs — sham'u (listened), yichelu (waited, hoped), yiddemu (were silent) — describe an audience hanging on Job's words. The atsati (my counsel) is the wisdom Job offered in the public square. This verse resumes the social portrait from verses 7-10 after the nature imagery of 18-20.

verses.text_hebrew: אַחֲרֵ֣י דְ֭בָרִי לֹ֣א יִשְׁנ֑וּ וְ֝עָלֵ֗ימוֹ תִּטֹּ֥ף מִלָּתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: After my words they spake not again; and my speech dropped upon them.
verses.rendering:
After I spoke, no one spoke again;
my words dropped gently on them.
translator_notes: Lo yishnu ('they did not repeat, did not speak again') means Job's word was final — no one contradicted or added to it. The verb tittof (dropped, dripped) uses rain imagery: Job's millah (word, speech) fell on them like gentle rain. The same verb appears in Deuteronomy 32:2 ('my teaching shall drop as the rain'). Job's speech was experienced as nourishing, not domineering.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִֽחֲל֣וּ כַמָּטָ֣ר לִ֑י וּ֝פִיהֶ֗ם פָּעֲר֥וּ לְמַלְקֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: And they waited for me as for the rain; and they opened their mouth wide as for the latter rain.
verses.rendering:
They waited for me as for rain;
they opened their mouths wide as for the spring showers.
translator_notes: The comparison intensifies: people waited for Job's speech ka-matar (like rain) — essential, life-giving, eagerly anticipated. The malqosh (late rain, spring rain) was the crucial rainfall that came just before harvest and determined whether crops would mature. Opening the mouth wide for it is the posture of desperate thirst being satisfied. Job's words were not merely respected but needed.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶשְׂחַ֣ק אֲ֭לֵהֶם לֹ֣א יַאֲמִ֑ינוּ וְא֥וֹר פָּ֝נַ֗י לֹ֣א יַפִּילֽוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: If I laughed on them, they believed it not; and the light of my countenance they cast not down.
verses.rendering:
When I smiled at them, they could hardly believe it;
the light of my face they would not let fall.
translator_notes: The verb eschaq ('I laughed, smiled') describes a gesture of approval. When Job smiled at someone, they could scarcely believe their good fortune — lo ya'aminu ('they did not believe it'). The or panai ('light of my face') is the same expression used of God's face in the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:25). No one would cause that light to fall or diminish — they treasured every sign of Job's favor.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶֽבְחַ֣ר דַּרְכָּם֮ וְאֵשֵׁ֪ב רֹ֥אשׁ וְ֭אֶשְׁכּוֹן כְּמֶ֣לֶךְ בַּגְּד֑וּד כַּאֲשֶׁ֖ר אֲבֵלִ֣ים יְנַחֵֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: I chose out their way, and sat chief, and dwelt as a king in the army, as one that comforteth the mourners.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final verse of Job's backward look captures the fullness of his former life in a triple image: leader, king, comforter. He chose their darkam (way, path) — he was the one who gave direction to his community. He sat as rosh (head, chief). He dwelt like a melekh ba-gedud (king among his troop) — not a tyrant but a commander who lives among his soldiers. And the final image is the most poignant: ka-asher avelim yenachem ('like one who comforts mourners'). Job's supreme role was not dominance but consolation. He was the person others turned to in grief. Now he is the one in grief, and his three friends have failed utterly at the task Job once performed effortlessly.
translator_notes: The combination of authority (choosing the way, sitting as head, dwelling as king) with tenderness (comforting mourners) makes this verse one of the most complete portraits of leadership in the Hebrew Bible. The gedud (troop, raiding band) suggests that Job's leadership operated in crisis situations, not just in peacetime governance. The chapter closes on the word yenachem ('he comforts') — the very thing Job's friends have failed to provide.
